rationale_for_baseline_tier: New acute bilateral lower-limb weakness and numbness with inability to bear weight, on a background of severe unremitting axial bone pain and constitutional decline, is a suspected malignant spinal cord / cauda equina compression — a time-critical oncological and neurosurgical emergency. The patient is non-ambulatory, alone, and on the floor after a fall, so cannot self-transport. Permanent paraplegia and irreversible sphincter dysfunction follow within hours without emergency imaging, corticosteroid and decompression. T5 (call 000, emergency ambulance) is the only appropriate tier; the definitive steroid, MRI and neurosurgical/radiotherapy care are delivered at hospital.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New or worsening bilateral leg weakness/numbness, saddle numbness, or loss of bladder/bowel control
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — call 000 now and do not wait to complete assessment
escalation_edges.rationale: Cardinal features of spinal cord / cauda equina compression; every hour of delay reduces the chance of neurological recovery.
escalation_edges.communication_template: The sudden weakness and numbness in both your legs is a serious emergency. I'm calling an ambulance for you now — please stay lying flat and still and don't try to stand or move.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Collapse, loss of consciousness, or new severe breathlessness/chest symptoms while waiting
escalation_edges.rationale: Any acute deterioration in an unwell, alone, immobile patient requires immediate paramedic resuscitation capability.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you feel like you're going to pass out, or your breathing becomes hard, tell 000 straight away — stay on the line with them.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New fever, rigors, or feeling systemically toxic (possible infection/sepsis on top of the malignancy)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same emergency pathway — mention any fever to paramedics/ED
escalation_edges.rationale: Superimposed infection or sepsis in a debilitated patient is itself an emergency and must not delay the primary escalation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a fever or shaking chills, tell the paramedics — but you already need the ambulance now regardless.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Patient is alone at home, on the floor, unable to get up
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Confirm the patient can unlock/allow entry for paramedics; if not, tell 000 so forced entry can be arranged. Do not ask the patient to move to open a door — keep them still and problem-solve access with the dispatcher.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Older adult, cachectic and frail with a high fall risk
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Immobility and fall risk reinforce the need for ambulance transport rather than any self-transport; a fall during the wait could cause a pathological fracture.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an ambulance right now and tell them you have sudden weakness and numbness in both legs and cannot stand, with severe back pain. Stay lying flat and completely still — do not try to get up, walk, or move to the bathroom. Keep warm, keep your phone beside you, and if you can, unlock the door or arrange for the paramedics to get in without you moving.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Call 000 immediately for an emergency ambulance
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 operates 24 hours a day — this is an emergency and must not wait for daytime clinic hours or a GP appointment.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide the paramedics and emergency department with the timeline of the back pain, the exact time the leg weakness and numbness began, the urinary symptoms, haematuria and weight loss. Hospital oncology/neurosurgery will lead ongoing care and communicate back to the GP.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating new bilateral leg weakness/back pain as a GP, urgent-care or outpatient-imaging problem rather than a 000 emergency.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Delay to decompression allows the cord lesion to become fixed, causing permanent paraplegia and irreversible loss of bladder/bowel control.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Directing a non-ambulatory patient who is alone on the floor to get to an emergency department by self/assisted transport instead of dispatching an ambulance — risks a fall/pathological fracture and delays time-critical treatment.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Over-triage is not a meaningful risk here; T5 is the correct emergency response.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: The underlying cause and the exact level/completeness of the cord lesion cannot be determined via telehealth, and the cancer diagnosis cannot be confirmed remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Act on the red-flag syndrome, not the uncertain cause: escalate to 000 for emergency assessment. The emergency response is identical regardless of which malignancy or lesion is responsible.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Withholding escalation until the cause is clearer, arranging outpatient investigation, or prematurely declaring a confirmed cancer diagnosis to the patient.
